description: A common bird throughout Belize, the Melodious Blackbird is often seen in towns, villages, and farmland. Males are entirely black with a glossy sheen, while females are duller brown-black.
funFact: Named for its melodious, flute-like song, often heard at dawn and dusk.

description: One of the most common hummingbirds in Central America. It has a green body, rufous tail, and straight red bill with a black tip.
funFact: These tiny birds can beat their wings up to 80 times per second!

description: A bold and noisy flycatcher often found in pairs or small groups. Has a distinctive white eyebrow, dark cap, and yellow belly.
funFact: Social Flycatchers build their nests near wasp nests for protection from predators.

description: A large, noisy jay with brown plumage, a pale belly, and a long tail. Often travels in small flocks through the forest canopy.
funFact: Brown Jays are highly social and live in cooperative family groups.

description: A small seedeater with sexual dimorphism: males have a distinctive black hood and white collar, while females are plain buffy-brown.
funFact: Males sing from exposed perches to attract females during breeding season.

description: A boldly patterned wren with heavy black spotting on white underparts and a distinctive barred tail often held upright.
funFact: This wren's loud, musical song is often performed as a duet between mated pairs.

description: The national bird of Belize! Unmistakable with its massive rainbow-colored bill, black plumage, yellow throat and chest, and red undertail.
funFact: Despite its large size, the bill is surprisingly light, made of hollow bone struts covered in keratin.

description: A skulking bird of the forest understory. Males are dull red with a brighter red throat patch, while females are olive-brown.
funFact: Often follows army ant swarms to catch insects fleeing from the ants.

description: A smaller relative of the toucan with a colorful bill, black head, yellow underparts with a black chest band, and red rump.
funFact: Aracaris often sleep communally, with up to six birds roosting together in a tree cavity.

description: A small, compact dove. Males are ruddy brown with a gray crown, while females are more uniformly gray-brown. Often seen on the ground.
funFact: This dove's soft, monotonous cooing is a common sound in open areas throughout Belize.

description: A tiny, colorful finch. Males have blue-black upperparts with a bright yellow forehead and underparts, while females are olive-green.
funFact: Euphonias are specialist mistletoe berry eaters and help spread these parasitic plants.

description: A large, thick-billed songbird with a distinctive black head, white throat, and olive-green back. Often heard singing from cover.
funFact: The name 'saltator' comes from Latin meaning 'dancer' or 'leaper'.

description: A tiny grassland bird. Males are entirely glossy blue-black, while females are plain brown. Males perform spectacular jumping displays.
funFact: Males leap straight up into the air while singing, sometimes jumping over 100 times per hour!

description: An all-black bird with a distinctive long tail and grooved, parrot-like bill. Often seen in small groups in open country.
funFact: Anis are cooperative breeders, with multiple females laying eggs in a single communal nest.

description: A small, mostly green parakeet with an olive throat and blue in the wings. Often seen in noisy flocks flying overhead.
funFact: These parakeets excavate nest cavities in termite mounds in trees.

description: A pale blue-gray tanager with distinctive yellow wing patches visible in flight. Often found in pairs or small groups.
funFact: This tanager often follows mixed-species feeding flocks through the forest.

description: A large, slender cuckoo with rufous upperparts, gray underparts, and a very long tail with white-tipped feathers.
funFact: Named for its squirrel-like movements as it hops through branches hunting for large insects.

description: A brown woodcreeper with heavy streaking on the head and underparts, and a pale ivory-colored, slightly curved bill.
funFact: Woodcreepers use their stiff tail feathers as a prop while climbing tree trunks.

description: A distinctive bird with pale gray plumage, black face and cap, red bare skin around the eye, and a pink-based bill.
funFact: Tityras nest in old woodpecker holes and often line their nests with leaves and bark.

description: A large flycatcher with a massive black bill, rufous-edged wings and tail, and yellow underparts. Similar to Social Flycatcher but larger.
funFact: The huge bill is an adaptation for catching large flying insects and even small fruits.

description: A small bird with a gray-blue crown and upper face, olive-green upperparts, whitish chin, pale gray throat, very pale lemon-yellow breast, and whitish belly with greenish yellow flanks.
funFact: Often joins mixed-species feeding flocks and sometimes hangs upside-down to feed.

description: A small flycatcher with a dark blackish gray crown, white eye-ring, sooty olive-gray back, dusky wings with two thin wing bars, white throat and chin, and white to yellowish white underparts.
funFact: Sits erect on exposed perches and catches insects in mid-air, returning to the same perch and shivers its tail upon landing.

description: Males have slate-black head, neck, and chest with a sky-blue eye ring, bright metallic bluish green to golden green upperparts, and cadmium to chrome yellow underparts. Females have dark slate upperparts instead of metallic green.
funFact: Nests in active termite mounds, with both parents excavating the nest cavity in termitaria.

description: Adults have a light bluish head and underparts, with darker blue upperparts and a shoulder patch colored a different shade of blue. Sexes are similar, but immature birds are much duller.
funFact: Often called blue jean in Trinidad and Tobago, this confiding species thrives around human habitation.

description: Adults have metallic bronze to bronzy green upperparts, with white underparts and metallic bronzy green from the cheeks to the flanks. The tail is metallic bronze with darker bands.
funFact: Males sing in leks and are believed to be polygynous, with females building cup nests decorated with lichen and moss.

description: Males have a yellow forehead, olive-green crown, nape, upperparts, and tail with a bluish gray gloss, and bright yellow underparts. Females have a rufous-chestnut forehead and mostly olive plumage with tawny-rufous underparts.
funFact: Builds a globe-shaped nest with a side entrance made from moss, roots, and plant fibers, typically placed in a clump of moss or epiphytes.

description: A very small wren with brownish plumage. The name troglodytes means hole dweller, referencing the bird's tendency to disappear into crevices when hunting insects or seeking shelter.
funFact: Formerly considered the same species as the Northern House Wren but was split based on genetic divergence, differences in vocalizations, and morphology.

description: Has chestnut brown upperparts with a darker crown, pale eyebrows, black-and-white streaked sides of the head and neck, and white underparts becoming buff on the lower belly. Wings and very short tail are barred with black.
funFact: Pairs often sing in duets, and the song has been compared to the opening bars of Beethoven's Fifth Symphony by ornithologist Luis Baptista.
